coredata.dc:title: Stability study of low-cost carbon quantum dots nanofluids with saline water and their application investigation for the performance improvement of solar still
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wenjing
Zhou, Jiaying
Zhang, Fan
Li, Xiaoke
Guo, Junyuan
coredata.dc:description: The poor stability of carbon nanofluids limits their application in solar desalination process, especially when nanoparticles need to contact with saline water directly. Herein, this study proposes a mild manufacturing route to prepare the highly stable carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofluids by microwave method coupling a simple dilution. The systematic stability evaluation was conducted and the results showed almost all of CQDs nanofluids could keep stable in saline water with a wide NaCl concentration range (from 0 to 50 g/L), different co-existing salt ions and organic matters. The high thermal stability of CQDs nanofluids also were proved and the variation of Turbiscan stability index (TSI) value was below 0.24 after five heating cycles. The optical properties were evaluated and nearly 100 % incoming solar energy was captured by 10 vol% CQDs nanofluids. The above results indicate the application potential of CQDs nanofluids in solar desalination process. Moreover, the performance of solar still with CQDs nanofluids was compared with the conventional solar still. The results show that the cumulative water productions are 521 mL in the conventional solar still and 823 mL in the solar still with CQDs nanofluids of an enhancement in the water productivity of 57.9 %. The average energy efficiency of solar still with or without CQDs nanofluids are 34.23 % and 21.52 %, respectively. In addition, the economic analysis was conducted and the results show water production cost is 0.013–0.015 $/L for solar still with CQDs nanofluids.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The poor stability of carbon nanofluids limits their application in solar desalination process, especially when nanoparticles need to contact with saline water directly. Herein, this study proposes a mild manufacturing route to prepare the highly stable carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofluids by microwave method coupling a simple dilution. The systematic stability evaluation was conducted and the results showed almost all of CQDs nanofluids could keep stable in saline water with a wide NaCl concentration range (from 0 to 50 g/L), different co-existing salt ions and organic matters. The high thermal stability of CQDs nanofluids also were proved and the variation of Turbiscan stability index (TSI) value was below 0.24 after five heating cycles. The optical properties were evaluated and nearly 100 % incoming solar energy was captured by 10 vol% CQDs nanofluids. The above results indicate the application potential of CQDs nanofluids in solar desalination process. Moreover, the performance of solar still with CQDs nanofluids was compared with the conventional solar still. The results show that the cumulative water productions are 521 mL in the conventional solar still and 823 mL in the solar still with CQDs nanofluids of an enhancement in the water productivity of 57.9 %. The average energy efficiency of solar still with or without CQDs nanofluids are 34.23 % and 21.52 %, respectively. In addition, the economic analysis was conducted and the results show water production cost is 0.013–0.015 $/L for solar still with CQDs nanofluids.

ce:section.ce:para: Freshwater scarcity, fossil fuel depletion and global warming all are the top risks of the 21st Century. As a blue planet, over 70 % area of earth is sea, so obtaining “free” water from sea seems to be an obvious solution. But, entirely relying on conventional desalination processes is not feasible because of their energy-extensive consumption and high CO2 emission [1]. In response to consume the minimum carbon footprint, using abundance solar resources to convert seawater to freshwater provides a promising strategy. In solar desalination processes, solar energy could be converted to chemical energy, thermal energy, and electrical energy directly for desalination without extra energy input [2]. With the recent development of light-harvesting materials, the solar-thermal conversion is put great hope and easy to implement, especially for realizing household or point of use potable water supply in the arid inland regions [3].

Solar desalination based on brine water distillation commonly involves three steps, namely solar energy conversion, vapor generation, and vapor condensation [4]. Therein, solar thermal conversion and vapor generation as the core process have been intensively studied. In the past few years, a new concept named “solar steam generation” was emerging, in which solar absorber as the most important component was successively forming two branches, namely volumetric solar absorber and floating solar absorber [5,6]. The idea of those absorbers starts from improving the evaporation of water by enhancing sunlight absorption and photo-thermal conversion. The former employs nanofluids to capture sunlight and heat surrounding water by suspended nanoparticles, and the latter designs the floating porous materials to absorb solar energy, heat water locally, and provide the channel for vapor transportation. Thanks to a great number of efforts on the development of solar absorbers, the high evaporation efficiency has been gotten. To be specific, the evaporation efficiency of gold nanofluids under concentrated sunlight within 5 min could reach 95 % [7]. Even though under natural sunlight (750 W/m2), the evaporation efficiency of graphene oxide/gold nanofluids still could be up to 59.2 % in 10 min [8].

While superior solar evaporation efficiency has been realized, there still exists a big gap between the current studies and actual application. Especially for floating materials, achieving desirable structures meanwhile require a lengthy development cycle, a complex fabrication process and high material cost [9]. Conversely, the preparation of nanofluids is easier and has more potential for the application in the small-scale desalination devices, such as the solar still [10,11]. Iqbal et al. reviewed the nanofluids-assisted solar still for desalination, and nanofluids were considered with promising economic and environmental benefits for improving desalination performance of solar still, of which Al2O3 was the most studied due to low cost and easy availability, followed carbon based materials also received intensive attention [12]. For example, Elango et al. employed Al2O3 nanofluids in the solar still with 29.95 % productivity and the water price were about 0.076 $/L [13]. While a large number of literatures have proved the application potential of nanofluids for the small-scale solar desalination devices [14]. However, those simple systems still face to some economic, environmental and energy conversion challenges [15]. To be specific, the choice of nanoparticles, the preparation of nanofluids, the stable photo-thermal conversion of absorbers in saline water, reusability of nanoparticles, and environmental impacts should be focused in the next stage of research work.

Recently, Carbon base materials gain a lot of attention to prepare nanofluids. Liu et al. found the solar steam generation efficiency of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanofluids could reach 47 % and prepared 1 L 100 mg/L rGO nanofluids spent about $0.06 [5]. Moreover, Zeiny et al. compared gold and carbon black nanofluids for solar evaporation and found that to reach 1 g/s vapor rate, gold nanofluids would cost 300 folds higher than carbon nanofluids [16]. For carbon based nanofluids-assisted solar still, the water price was close to 0.03 $/L [17]. Therefore, carbon base materials have the potential as cheap alternatives to noble metal materials. However, the hard dispersion and instability in base fluid is the main obstacle for the application of carbon materials. Our previous research attempted modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) by macromolecule polymer to enhance the dispersion and stability of MWCNTs nanofluids, but the discussion of the economic cost was weakened [18].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an emerging class of carbon base materials are defined as quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with diameters in 3–20 nm ([19]. The special size and carbonaceous nature endow them many advantages. Due to their nontoxicity, biocompatibility, and strong fluorescence, CQDs were widely applied in medicine and biology [20]. Moreover, abundant hydrophilic groups on their surfaces (O content could occupy 5 % to 50 % in weight) ensure their good dispersion and stability in polar solution. Therefore, aiming to the challenges of nanofluids with respect to hard dispersion, instability, expensive nano-materials, and environmental risk, CQDs was firstly discussed as nanofluids for heat transfer process in 2018 [21]. Whereafter, Seifikar et al. synthesized polyethylene glycol based CQDs nanofluids as solar thermal conversion fluids to heat water, and the increment of water temperature could keep at 6 °C within 30 min [22]. Besides, the stability of ethylene glycol based CQDs nanofluids displayed a low sensitivity in temperature change [23].

In light of these recent advances, it is found that CQDs nanofluids display the superior thermal stability, thermal conductivity property and solar thermal conversion performance and have the potential in the application of solar desalination process. As volumetric solar absorber, nanoparticles need to contact with saline water directly, the salt effect on the suspension stability becomes considerable, which will impact the whole performance of desalination in the late stage of operation. However, the systematic discussion of salt effect is only beginning in existing researches. Hence, in this study, we attempted to synthesize CQDs nanofluids with high stability from polyethylene glycol-200 (PEG-200) by microwave method coupling a simple dilution. The whole preparation process is simple, no need of special equipment and suitable for large-scale production. The effect of seawater components for nanofluids stability was put emphasis on discussion and a possible effect mechanism of salt ions on CQDs nanofluids stability was proposed. The results show that CQDs nanofluids display the superior stability in different medium. In addition, the optical property, the water production of solar still with seawater based CQDs nanofluids and the economic analysis were carried out and the results prove this system with a higher water productivity, energy efficiency and low-costing has the application prospect in the solar desalination.

Polyethylene glycol (PEG, 200 MW), sodium chloride (NaCl, purity >98 %), potassium chloride (KCl, purity >98 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2, purity >98 %) and magnesium chloride (MgCl2, purity >98 %) were purchased from KeLong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). All reagents were used without further purification.

The preparation of CQDs was based on a microwaved assisted pyrolysis method [24]. Herein, PEG-200 was selected as raw material, which was non-toxic, inexpensive, and common in food and toiletries as additive. To be specific, 75 mL PEG-200 was added in a 250 mL reactor and then exposed under microwave irradiation with 700 W for 5, 10, 20, and 30 min. As increasing irradiation time, the obvious color change was observed by the eye, attributing to the generation of CQDs as shown in Fig. 1(a). Moreover, Fig. 1(b) and (c) showed the average temperature and the temperature distribution of each sample took by an infrared imaging device. It can be found that the average temperature of each sample was higher than 200 °C, and the highest surface temperature of each sample was lower than 280 °C. Hence, PEG-200 was regarded as the main base fluid for CQDs slurry because the heat decomposition temperature of PEG-200 is 297 °C.

CQDs nanofluids were prepared by a modified two-step method, which was similar to our previous works [25]. In brief, prepared CQDs slurry in Section 2.2 was diluted by deionized water at 5 to 30 vol%, and then mixed by mechanical stirring. Obtained uniform suspension as solar absorbers was discussed as CQDs nanofluids. Such treated method could apply CQDs directly without cleaning, drying, and re-dispersing, and residual PEG-200 from raw slurry could act as a stabilizer to improve CQDs stability. Hence, no extra surfactant was added.

For the batch experiments of salt effect, different inorganic salts were prepared as the stock solutions with a certain concentration before CQDs nanofluids preparation. And then, the different salt (NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, and NaHCO3) stock solutions with different concentrations were used to dilute CQDs slurry and mixed by mechanical stirring to obtain saline water based nanofluids. Moreover, humic acid (HA) was added directly in nanofluids with constantly stirring to ensure HA dissolved.

The morphologies of dry CQDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G220 microscope). The UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV 3802, UNICO (Shanghai), China) was used to measure the absorption spectra of CQDs nanofluids at room temperature. The spectrofluorimeter (HITACHI High-Technologies Corporation, Japan) was employed to determine the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CQDs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CQDs were obtained by a FT-IR spectrophotometer (WQF-520, Beijing Ruili, China). Zeta potential value was measured by a Zeta potential measurer (Brookhaven Instruments Corporation, USA). The particle size was determined by a laser particle sizer (Master sizer 2000, Malvern Instruments, UK) with a dynamic light scattering method (DLS). Each sample was measured at least 3 experimental repetitions, and the error range was kept at ± 10 %.

The in-situ visual inspection was used to reflect the long-term stability of CQDs slurry and saline water based nanofluids qualitatively. All samples were left on a shelf for three months and took images with a camera to observe the generation of precipitation. Meanwhile, during the standing period, 1 mL supernatant of each CQDs slurry sample was taken off to measure the carbon content at intervals as a quantitative method to reflect the concentration change of suspension. Thermogravimetry was employed to evaluate the carbon content in solution by a synchronous integrated thermal analyzer (STA 449F3, Netzsch, Germany) and the detailed method was described in [25]. In brief, 1 mL supernatant of sample was dialyzed (1000 Da) in deionized water for 24 h to remove extra PEG-200 and dialysis solution was condensed by the reduced pressure distillation. Then, the condensed solution was measured by the thermal analyzer with heat temperature from 25 to 800 °C at rate of 20 °C/min under argon atmosphere. Based on the loss weight curves to calculate the CQDs content and the mass balance was used to check the calculated results.

Turbiscan stability analyzer (Formulation, France) was employed to observe the stability of the sample at 25 °C and the whole monitoring time was 1 h. To be specific, the fresh-prepared CQDs nanofluids were added in a 20 mL glass sample cell and kept the liquid level about 50 mm. Then, a pulsed near infrared light with 880 nm passed through the sample cell and the detector would receive and record the transmittance and backscattering single at an angle of 180° and 135° to the light source, respectively. The transmittance could reflect the transparent or weakly turbid phase and backscattering single could reflect the opaque phase. By scanning from bottom to neck of sample cell at 20 s/scan, the change of transmittance and backscattering with different depth of suspension could be draw and those vertical profiles could reflect the state of suspensions visually.

Subsequently, Turbiscan Stability Index (TSI) was calculated to describe the stability of suspensions quantitatively as follows:
(1)

is the ith scan and (i-1)th scan at a given high of h, respectively. H is the height from bottom to the neck of sample and i is scan number from 1 to k (k = total time/scan speed). To reduce the interfacial interference, the TSI of a middle zone (13 to 28 mm) was recorded by Turbiscan stability analyzer.

The thermal stability of CQDs nanofluids was conducted by a cyclic heating method. Specifically, CQDs nanofluids was heated from room temperature to 80 °C at increasing rate of 5 °C /min and then cooled to room temperature. The above process was regarded as one cycle, and five cycle was carried out for one sample. When all heating cycles were finished, the sample was transferred to the Turbiscan stability analyzer after returning to room temperature. After standing 1 h, TSI value was measured by three times for each sample and the average values were used to show the thermal stability of CQDs nanofluids.

A simple solar desalination system was applied to discuss the performance improvement by CQDs nanofluids. Specifically, a single basin solar still was employed, which had the same design as our previous work as shown in Fig. 2 . It is worthy to note that the thermal insulation layer was moved from the major structure to make the real photo of solar still in Fig. 2 (b) more visual. The basin area of solar still is 0.25 m2 and the slop angle of glass plate covering on solar still is 30°. More detailed information about this type of device can be found in [18].

The solar desalination tests were conducted under the natural sunlight and the weather condition was a clear and sunny day during June to September in 2020. Two identical solar stills were added with fresh seawater and seawater based CQDs nanofluids, respectively and the depth of basin water was set at 1 cm. The K-type thermocouples were used to record the basin water and condensation cover temperatures. The pyranometer (MS-602, EKO) was set horizontally on the top of device to measure the solar radiation intensity. The anemometer (AT-816, SMART SENSOR) was used to recorded the wind speed. The pure water yield was recorded in every 1 h interval from 9 am to 18 pm and the average values were presented.

Moreover, the uncertainty analysis of solar desalination test was performed based on systemic errors of each instrument and calculated as follows [26]:
(2)

where u is the standard uncertainty and a is the accuracy of each instrument from manufacturers. The calculated results are presented Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis, morphology, and characteristic of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Various procedures have been developed to fabricate CQDs, which can be classified by top-down and bottom-up routes [27]. The former aims to generate CQDs from a larger carbon structure (graphite or CNTs) by some energy intensive techniques such as laser ablation, arc-discharge or proton-beam irradiation [28]. Obviously, expensive raw materials, sophisticated equipment and complex processes involved in the top-down route are unlikely to be extended in some fields with low economic returns. Conversely, the bottom-up route brings enticing opportunity, which synthesizes CQDs from citrate or saccharide molecular by chemical synthesis, pyrolysis, hydrothermal and solvothermal treatment [29]. While raw material cost can be reduced, the preparation processes of CQDs still are energy-consuming and time-consuming [20]. In this study, PEG-200 as the solution was heated directly by a microwave oven to produce CQDs in minutes, which didn't require tough reaction conditions and uncommon equipment. Well-dispersed CQDs in PEG-200 could be used directly by dilution to prepare nanofluids. Moreover, CQDs slurry could be transported and stored easily. Fresh CQD nanofluids could be applied on the spot. This fast and less expensive method is more beneficial to nanofluids application in some remote areas.

To confirm the valid generation of CQDs, a series of experiments were carried out. Firstly, the morphology and size distribution of isolated CQDs were observed by TEM. It can be found that CQDs (5 min) morphology is near spheroidal (Fig. 3(a)), and the corresponding size distribution is in the range of 8 to 24 nm and concentrates in 12 nm (Fig. 3(c)). With treated time increasing, CQDs size increases as shown in Fig. 3(b) and (d), which is similar with the previous studies about CQDs synthesis by microwave [24,30]. While the morphology of CQDs is unchanged, the size distribution of CQDs (30 min) increases and concentrates in 20 to 24 nm.

Fig. 4 presents the fluorescent properties of CQDs. In Fig. 4(a), under UV light, PEG-200 keeps transparency and CQDs sample displays blue light, which confirms the generation of CQDs with fluorescence roughly. The absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra for CQDs (30 min) are depicted in Fig. 4(b) and Fig. 4(c), respectively. Noticeably, there is a strong absorption peak in 280 nm in Fig. 4(b), which is similar to the result in previous study [30]. Fig. 4(c) depicts the PL spectra of sample excited from 360 to 460 nm and the similar shape of curves can be observed starting at 380 to 480 nm. The maximum PL intensity can be found at 445 nm, when the sample is excited at 360 nm, with a 168 nm red shift from the absorption peak, which also was found in [30]. Moreover, to verify the generation of CQDs in all samples, the PL spectra of CQDs synthesized by different treatment time were measured upon excitation at 360 nm as shown in Fig. 4(d). Clearly, under excitation at 360 nm, all samples display the strong fluorescence emission peaks at 445 nm and the PL intensity of CQDs is impacted by microwave treatment time. With treatment time increasing, the fluorescence intensity increases except of 30 min sample, which can be attributed to the size increase of CQDs as discussed in Fig. 3. When the treated time is under 20 min, the increasing concentration of CQDs plays the main role for PL intensity, but when the treated time increases to 30 min, the size of CQDs will impact PL intensity significantly.

Apart from the fluorescent properties of CQDs, the thermo-physical properties of CQDs slurry and nanofluids also were measured as shown in Table S1 and the measurement methods of each parameter also were presented in Supplementary Information: Section 1. With increase of treatment time, the thermal conductivity, density and specific heat of CQDs slurries increase, which indicates the generation of CQDs can enhance the thermo-physical properties of base fluid. However, the viscosity of CQDs slurries decreased with the treatment time, which could because the long-chain molecular structure and hydrogen bonds broke in PEG-200 under the effect of microwave. On the other hand, with decrease of viscosity, the motion speed of nanoparticle would increase in base fluid causing the enhancement of thermal conductivity properties. This phenomenon also occurs in other carbon base nanofluids with polymer base fluids [31]. Moreover, the thermo-physical properties of CQDs nanofluids also were summarized in Table S1. Compared with base fluid (water), the thermal conductivity and specific heat of CQDs nanofluids decreased and the viscosity and density of CQDs nanofluids increased. It indicates that CQDs can play the positive effect on the enhancement of thermal conductivity, but PEG-200 plays more obvious effect on the thermo-physical properties of fluids [32].

Fig. 5 shows that the FT-IR spectra of PEG-200 and CQDs. Apart from the group frequencies at 3431, 2940, 1736 and 1130 cm−1 derived from PEG-200, more peaks were detected in CQDs [33]. The peak at 3743 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of alcoholic hydroxyl group (-OH), which might cause by the generation of -OH on CQDs surface [23]. The peak at 1643 cm−1 indicates the presence of CO [34]. Combining with the in-plane bending vibration of -OH (1440 cm−1) and the stretching vibration of CO (1300 cm−1), the carboxylic groups could form on CQDs surface [35]. Those abundant hydrophilic groups present on the surface of CQDs endowing their high dispersive capacity and stability in water.

Nanofluids as the direct solar absorber, their stability was directly related to their performance in different application scenarios. For example, Parsa et al., found the stability of nanofluids prepared by one-step method was higher than by two-step method, and their performance in the photovoltaic/thermal system was substantially improved than two-step nanofluids [36]. Hence, combined with the application background, the stability of CQDs slurry and diluted CQDs nanofluids are discussed systematically in this part.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The long-term of stability for CQDs slurry
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the long-term stability test was conducted for CQDs slurry and the carbon content in the supernatant liquid of sample was measured as an evaluation index. The changes of carbon content in supernatant liquid for CQDs slurry with different microwave treatment times were shown in Fig. 6 . Obviously, the changes of all CQDs slurry samples were slight within 90 days. While the 30 min sample has the largest particles size distribution range, the carbon content only changes from 0.913 to 0.899 g/L after standing for 90 days reflected that 30 min sample still could keep stable. For other samples, the changes of carbon content are almost below 0.01 g/L. Hence, CQDs slurry owns good stability with potential for long-time storage and transportation. In the actual application, CQDs slurry could be used as concentrate raw material produced by the manufacturer, which could avoid the complex preparation process for nanofluids in situ. Moreover, for 30 min sample, PEG-200 could generate sufficient CQDs about 0.9 g/L with high stability, which was regarded as a feasible method to produce nanofluids with high efficiency and stability.

Zeta potential analysis was employed to predict the stability of nanofluid [37]. The hydrophilic function groups on CQDs surface are a reason for the good stability, which is attributed to the surface forces balance among nanoparticles in theory [38]. By measuring the electrophoretic mobility, the electric charge repulsive forces could be represented by Zeta potential value. Hence, according to Fig. 7(a), it can be found that with the increase of NaCl concentration from 0 to 50 g/L, the Zeta potential values of all samples present a decreasing tendency. Among them, the effect of NaCl concentration is the weakest for 5 min sample, whose Zeta potential value drops from 52.6 to 43.3 mV, whereas the strongest for that of 30 min sample drops from 46.2 to 14.2 mV. It indicates that the effect of ionic strength is significant for the stability of CQDs nanofluids with higher carbon concentrations and larger CQDs size. The classical DLVO theory could address that further, which considers the balance of interparticle interaction potentials only by Van der Waals (vdW) attraction and electrical double layer (EDL) repulsion [39]. Based on DLVO, with the increase of ionic strength, EDL will be compressed and repulsion will reduce to smaller than vdW attractive force, so CQDs aggregation will generate [40]. However, for CQDs nanofluids prepared from a shorter microwave treatment time, the particle size is smaller. In terms of the expression for vdW interactions, the particle diameter is in direct proportion to vdW interactions, so for smaller particles, vdW attractive force is smaller. Moreover, the structural interactions also can explain this result [39]. With a shorter treatment time, CQDs concentration is lower and residual PEG-200 is higher, which can act as surfactant coating on CQDs surface, forming the steric effect to increase the stability of CQDs nanofluids [41]. Commonly, when the Zeta potential value was higher than 30 mV, the suspension system could be regarded as stable. Hence, all of fresh CQDs nanofluids prepared with pure water own good stability. With increasing NaCl concentration, the Zeta potential values of CQDs nanofluids still keep over 30 mV, except of 30 min sample, indicating CQDs nanofluids can withstand the impact of salt ions. In addition, the DLS particle size of all samples was also measured as shown in Fig. 7(a). Increasing NaCl concentration from 0 to 50 g/L, the particles size of all samples displays an increasing tendency, of which the increase of particles size obviously becomes larger in 30 min samples, from 90.42 to 320.79 nm. Therefore, the larger size of aggregations generation might be the reason for the destabilization of CQDs nanofluids. Moreover, pH effect on the Zeta potential of CQDs nanofluids was discussed. As shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Information), the Zeta potential values of CQDs nanofluids still keep over 35 mV at pH 3 to 11, indicating that pH effect is not significant for the stability of CQDs nanofluids, except of the extreme acid condition. The unstable region occurred at pH 1, in which the minimum Zeta potential value was 26.6 mV for 30 min sample.

To verify the prediction of Zeta potential analysis, the standing tests were conducted. TSI was chosen as an evaluation index, which was calculated from situ monitoring optical data for each sample. Fig. 7(b) shows the TSI value of CQDs nanofluids with different NaCl concentrations after standing 1 h. With increasing NaCl concentration, the TSI value increases gradually and the influence of NaCl is stronger to the samples with higher CQDs concentration. For example, TSI value increases from 0.182 to 3.924 for 30 min sample, when NaCl concentration increases from 0 to 50 g/L. In contrast, for 5 min sample, TSI value only changes from 0.131 to 0.782. Additionally, TSI value can quantize the different states of the suspension system. When TSI value is below 0.5, a good stability is considered for the suspension system without significant variation. When TSI value is between 0.5 and 3, a weak stability is considered with emerging invisible destabilization and the dynamic stability of small aggregation in suspension emerges. When TSI value is over 3, a significant destabilization happens. Hence, it is confirmed that although a high concentration of NaCl is introduced, the whole the system still could keep basically stable for 5 to 20 min samples. Nanofluids with low CQDs concentration and high content of PEG-200 can keep stable under a high salt concentration.

However, as a solar absorber, a high particles concentration benefits nanofluid to capture more solar energy in theory. Hence, understanding the critical concentration of NaCl concentration and the destabilization process of 30 min samples is meaningful. For this purpose, TSI kinetic curves are discussed as shown in Fig. 7(c). For 30 min sample, the TSI value increases from 0 to 3.924 within 1 h at 50 g/L NaCl, meaning that the high NaCl concentration would affect the system stability significantly. Based on their trend, TSI value would continue to rise until visible precipitation occurring in system. However, when NaCl concentration is below 10 g/L, the whole system still could keep stable and TSI value could remain below 2 within 1 h.

Apart from NaCl effect, there are other types of inorganic ions and natural organic matters (NOMs) in the natural seawater. Based on seawater component characteristics, the effect of CaCl2, MgCl2, NaHCO3 and HA was discussed [42]. It is worth mentioning that some studies referred that HA as one of NOMs could improve the stability of particles in water. So, unlike other effect factors, HA was added in the sample with 10 g/L NaCl [42]. As shown in Fig. 7(d), with the addition of HA, the TSI values of all samples decrease blow to 1.5, indicating that HA could improve the stability of CQDs. HA could adsorb on the surface of nanoparticles to form steric hindrance to counteract EDL compression caused by salt ions [43]. Hence, some NOMs from the natural water body could act positive effect on CQDs nanofluids stability. Moreover, among all cations, the effect of Ca2+ for CQDs nanofluids stability is the most obvious, of which TSI value is 0.624, 1.553, 1.923 and 2.523 for 5 to 30 min sample, respectively. It is because that Ca2+ as divalent cations carried the stronger electrical charges will cause a stronger influence on the electrostatic equilibrium of nanofluids than other cations. Similar result also was found in Wu's work, in which 1 wt% CaCl2 solution would cause the form of micron-sized particles [44]. Moreover, the particle size grew >1000 nm in their work. But in our work, the particle size of fresh CQDs nanofluids is still in nanoscale, which could be explained by the different preparation method and PEG-200 might act as stabilizer. Besides, the anions effect is not obvious for CQDs nanofluids stability. As shown in Fig. 7(d), the changes of TSI value for all samples could be ignored with HCO3 −. All up, in the natural water body, the negative effect of Ca2+ is significant for CQDs nanofluids and even than Na+ under the same concentration, so the hardness of seawater needs to be considered in real application.

Finally, a long-term standing test was carried out with 10 vol% CQDs nanofluids diluting from 30 min sample with different NaCl concentrations and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . As Fig. 8(a) depicted, after standing 90 days, the transmittance of CQDs nanofluids with 1 g/L NaCl was between that of 5 min and 10 min CQDs slurry and the transmittance of CQDs nanofluids with NaCl over 5 g/L was between that of 5 min CQDs slurry and pure water. Hence, unlike other cases as reported, CQDs nanofluids are not failure completely for absorbing sunlight after destabilization happened [45]. Fig. 8(b) displays the digital photo taken after standing 7 days (up) and 90 days (down). An obvious sediment only occurs in CQDs nanofluids with 50 g/L NaCl at the first week. After standing 90 days, there is no change of CQDs nanofluids with 1 g/L NaCl. Hence, the operating period of CQDs nanofluids can adjust based on the salinity of water.

According to static analysis, particle size distribution, optical analysis and a long-term stable test, the precipitation formation mechanism of CQDs nanofluids with NaCl can be described as Scheme 1 . The precipitation formation process can be concluded in three stages. In the initial phase, with the effect of charged ions, the EDL of CQDs will be compressed and the system still keeps a dynamic stability due to electrostatic interaction. In the second phase, some loose CQDs aggregates generated with larger size would float up due to solution density, and meanwhile the smaller one would remain stable in the original position. In the last phase, with continued compression by charged ions, aggregates would become compact and the buoyancy of large CQDs aggregates would not resist the gravity, dropping to the bottom rapidly. Finally, the precipitation is found in the bottom while there still are some suspending CQDs.

Thermal stability is important for the direct solar desalination system with CQDs nanofluids, because with the change of natural condition, a cycling heating process could happen in CQDs nanofluids and endanger the lifetime of nanofluids [46]. Fig. 9 shows the TSI value change of CQDs nanofluids before and after heating cycling. After heating cycling, all of TSI values present an increasing trend, in which the increasing range of 50 vol% CQDs nanofluids are the largest about 0.24. Indeed, temperature would impact the kinetical stability of CQDs nanofluids. When the thermal energy of particles increases, the energy barrier of dispersion would reduce, which would lead to the increase of collision frequency for particles and ultimately induce the form of clusters and deposition [39]. However, after heating cycling, the TSI value of CQDs nanofluids still keeps below 0.8, in which the highest 50 vol% CQDs nanofluids only increase from 0.41 to 0.65. It can be explained that the formed cluster is mainly induced by Brownian motion and those cluster are considered with sufficient energy barrier to resist the thermal energy of most particles, and ultimately the whole system reach could be described as kinetical stable [47]. Hence, after heating cycling, CQDs would form clusters but the deposition with a not significant rate to keep stable within a long-term timeframe.

The sunlight absorption capacity is one of key factors to ensure the high solar-vapor conversion rate for nanofluids. To show the application potential of CQDs nanofluids in solar desalination system, the optical property of CQDs nanofluids was discussed. The extinction coefficient of CQDs nanofluids in the wavelength range from 190 to 1100 nm is shown in Fig. 10 . With the increase of microwave heating time, the extinction coefficient increases with the same dilution ratio. The average extinction coefficient increases from 0.18 (water) to 3.24. Moreover, the grew shadow in Fig. 10 represents the global total spectral irradiance spectra referred from [48]. It is obvious that CQDs nanofluids could enhance the solar absorption capacity in UV and Vis regions, effectively.

While CQDs own the excellent capacity to capture the incident light at UV and Vis regions, the light absorption capacity of CQDs nanofluids is not desired in NIR region [49]. However, it can be solved by adjusting CQDs proportion. The transmittance of CQD nanofluids with different volume fraction was calculated based on Beer-Lambert Law as shown in Fig. 11(a) [50]. Obviously, with CQDs volume fractions increasing from 0 to 50 %, the transmittance of nanofluids decreases from 87 % to 6.8 % at 900 nm. It means that the sunlight capture capacity of CQDs nanofluids is tailorable with change of CQDs concentration. Moreover, to measure the solar energy absorption ability of CQDs nanofluids, the absorbed energy fraction (F) is calculated based on Eq. (3), and the results are shown in Fig. 11(b).

Compared with pure water, nearly 100 % incoming solar energy can be captured by adjusting the depth of 10 vol% CQDs nanofluids. However, a higher thickness of medium means a higher heat loss. Hence, the solar desalination system commonly prefers to employ a thin brine water layer. When the thickness of nanofluids is at 1 cm, F is 85.78 % to 98.93 % for CQDs nanofluids diluting from 5 to 30 min slurry, which means that there still is >85 % solar energy which could be absorbed by CQDs at an effective thickness, which is higher than that of pure water (24.64 %).

To assess the solar desalination performance of CQDs nanofluids, the real seawater from Bohai Bay in Tianjin was used to dilute 30 min CQDs slurry for obtaining 10 vol% nanofluid and the seawater components are tabulated in Table 2 . The total salt ions concentration is lower than 20 g/L, so corresponding to the stability results in Section 3.2, CQDs nanofluids could keep stable. The solar desalination performance tests were conducted in the lab building roof in Chengdu University of Technology and the detailed weather conditions are shown in Fig. S2. The maximum solar intensity was 780 W/m2 at 13:00 pm and the daily average wind was 1.43 m/s. Moreover, Fig. 12(a) depicts the average air temperature was 29 °C, and the maximum temperature reaches to 32.4 °C around 14:00 pm. Fig. 12(a) also shows the variations of the basin water temperature in the solar stills with and without nanofluids. The hourly basin water temperatures of the solar still with CQDs nanofluids are more than that of the conventional solar still by 0.5–6.6 °C, and the maximum temperature difference happens around 13:00–14:00 pm. Combined with the variation data of solar intensity, it could be attributed that CQDs nanofluids display the higher solar energy absorption and photothermal conversion capacity under a higher solar energy incoming. Moreover, the condensation temperature also is important for water production in the solar still. During the evaporation process, the accumulated vapor will increase cover temperature and impact condensation rate of water. Hence, the variation of cover temperature was measured as shown in Fig. 12(a). It is found that the difference between basin water and cover temperature ranges from 1.5 to 12.2 °C and 0.5–10.1 °C in case of solar still with CQDs nanofluids and pure saline water, respectively. It could deduce that CQDs nanofluids as basin water could enhance the natural circulation inside the closed chamber by their special enhancement in heat and mass transfer [21,52]. As the result, the solar still owns higher evaporation rate with CQDs nanofluids. Besides, the hourly variations for water production and the total water production of a traditional solar still and a solar still with seawater based CQDs nanofluids are both shown in Fig. 12(b). Obviously, the water production of solar still with CQDs nanofluids is higher than traditional solar still. Corresponding to the highest basin water temperature and cover/basin water temperature difference, the highest hourly productions reach to 94 and 143 mL at 14:00 pm for solar still with seawater and CQDs nanofluids, respectively. The cumulative water productions are 521 and 823 mL at 18:00 pm in solar stills with seawater and CQDs nanofluids. Moreover, the daily energy efficiencies of solar still with or without CQDs nanofluids were 21.52 % and 34.23 %, respectively, which was calculated based on Eq. (4) [53].

The abovementioned results indicate that replacing basin water with CQDs nanofluids can achieve a higher water production efficiency with 57.9 % increase and a higher energy efficiency with 12.71 % increase in the traditional solar still. A comparison between the productivity improvement of traditional solar still with CQDs and other nanofluids were summarized in Table 3 . The results prove that the higher stability and sunlight absorption capacity CQDs nanofluids endow their potential to improve solar still productivity.

Besides, the quality of collected water was measured, in which the TDS value is 50 mg/L, the pH value decreases to 7.1 and TOC is around 2.12 mg/L, which means that PEG-200 will not escape from basin water during distillation process. The results of distilled water quality can meet the demand of World Health Organization (WHO, TDS < 1 mg/L) for drink water.

Moreover, the operation days is set at 300 days per year, neglecting the seasonal influence. This approximation process is common in the other works for the cost analysis of solar still [59]. Hence, the annual water productivity can be calculated as 625.2 and 987.6 L/m2/year for the solar still with pure saline water and CQDs nanofluids, respectively. The results are presented in Table 5 and the water production price of traditional solar still is 0.022 $/L higher than that of solar still with CQDs nanofluids. There are two schemes to calculate CQDs nanofluids cost with solar still. In the first one fitting with the low salinity water (around 1 mg/L), CQDs nanofluids can be replaced in every month. Considering the accumulation of salt, the residence time of CQDs nanofluids in basin is set on one month, and the cost of water production is 0.013 $/L. For high salinity water, the residence time of CQDs nanofluids is set on one week. It means that CQDs nanofluids need to be replaced every week, but water production cost only increases to 0.015 $/L, owing to a low cost of CQDs nanofluids. According to Asian market report, the price of PEG-200 fluctuated around 1719 $/MT in 2021 [60], and considering the loss cost of PEG-200 after treatment, the price of CQDs slurry is 2.9 $/kg. Compared with other metal or metallic oxide nanoparticles such as TiO2 (472.3 $/kg) summarized in Table 6 [61], CQDs slurry owns an overwhelming price advantage, and the water production cost of solar still with CQDs nanofluids is relatively low. Besides, in Essa's work, graphene quantum dots also was introduced in a solar still modified by a rotating wick belt and the highest increment of productivity with quantum dots nanofluid was 3 % [62]. However, distinguishing from present work, it mainly focused on the structure design of solar still and more detailed information about quantum dots was lacking.
